nativeRange.habitat: Rocky mbuna habitat along the shores of Taiwan Reef (Chinyankwazi Island area), Lake Malawi; upper rocky zones with sediment-free rubble and algae-encrusted boulders

decorPreferences: extensive rockwork with caves and crevices (mbuna-style stacking)
fine sand substrate (Lake Malawi cichlid mix or aragonite)
minimal or no live plants (will be uprooted)
open sandy areas between rock piles

diet.notes: Primarily aufwuchs-based: algae, biofilm, and small invertebrates scraped from rocks in the wild. In captivity, feed spirulina-based flakes or pellets as the staple (60–70% of diet); supplement with high-quality cichlid pellets, blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini), and occasional frozen or live brine shrimp, daphnia, or Mysis shrimp. Avoid high-protein foods such as bloodworms or beef heart — excess protein can cause Malawi bloat, a potentially fatal digestive condition in mbuna.

goodWith: other small to mid-sized mbuna of similar aggression (Labidochromis caeruleus, Iodotropheus sprengerae)
other Chindongo species where colour does not clash
Synodontis catfish (Lake Tanganyika synos work well as dither fish)
larger, robust Malawi cichlids that are not overly aggressive
avoidWith: visually similar blue-barred mbuna (interbreeding risk — males court females of similar-looking species)
other yellow Lake Malawi cichlids (female confusion)
Haplochromis / Aulonocara (different aggression dynamic, fin-nipping risk)
small or slow-moving fish that cannot defend themselves
invertebrates (shrimp and snails will be eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image — male showing blue body with dark vertical bars. Standard reference photo.
Aquarium shot showing male coloration; listed under old genus Pseudotropheus saulosi.

summary: A dwarf mbuna endemic to Taiwan Reef, Lake Malawi. Males are vivid blue with dark vertical bars; females are bright yellow — one of the most striking examples of sexual dichromatism in freshwater fish. Among the more manageable mbuna for intermediate cichlid keepers.
careNotes: Chindongo saulosi (formerly Pseudotropheus saulosi, reclassified by Li et al. in 2016) is a dwarf mbuna endemic to the restricted rocky habitat around Taiwan Reef (Chinyankwazi Island) in Lake Malawi. Adults reach only 7–10 cm, making them one of the smallest mbuna species and one of the most feasible for a 200 L setup, though larger tanks are always preferable for managing aggression. Sexual dichromatism is extreme: males are bright blue with 6–8 dark vertical bars, while females and juveniles are uniformly yellow — this is an unusually striking trait even by cichlid standards. A ratio of one male to at least two or three females is strongly recommended to distribute male aggression; a species-only or mbuna-community setup is advised. Maintain hard, alkaline water (pH 7.8–8.6, gH 10–20) with good filtration and regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly). Rockwork is essential — provide dense stacking with caves and territories. Avoid mixing with visually similar blue-barred mbuna as hybridisation is a serious concern in captivity, both for conservation and captive stock integrity. The species is listed as Critically Endangered (IUCN) due to its extremely restricted range (a single reef) and heavy collection pressure for the ornamental trade; aquarists are encouraged to source tank-bred specimens wherever possible. Malawi bloat is the primary disease risk — feed a low-protein, plant-based diet and avoid high-protein foods such as bloodworms.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning occurs readily in a well-maintained mbuna setup. The female collects fertilised eggs in her buccal cavity immediately after laying and incubates them for approximately 21–28 days. Broods are typically 10–25 fry. Females should ideally be moved to a separate holding tank in the final days of incubation to protect her and the fry from male aggression. Fry are released as miniature juveniles and begin as yellow (like females); males colour up to blue between 3–6 months of age. Fry accept crushed flake, micro-pellets, and newly-hatched brine shrimp nauplii from release. Stripping (manually removing fry from the female’s mouth after 18–21 days and raising in a tumbler) is common in commercial breeding but is not required for hobbyist success.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Chindongo saulosi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~10 cm), temperature (23–27°C), pH (7.5–8.5), hardness, native range (Taiwan Reef, Lake Malawi), diet (aufwuchs/algae-based omnivore), mouthbrooder confirmation, care and breeding notes, IUCN status reference, and reclassification to Chindongo (Li et al. 2016).

additional.name: FishBase — Chindongo saulosi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult TL (males to 9 cm, females to 7 cm), temperature range, taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range, depth range, and IUCN Critically Endangered status (range-restricted to single reef, overcollection). Confirmed current valid name Chindongo saulosi.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Mbuna Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade and husbandry reference. Confirmed hobbyist availability, tank size recommendations (75+ gal / ~280 L for mbuna communities; 55 gal / ~200 L acceptable for dwarf species), diet guidance (spirulina-heavy, protein-restriction for Malawi bloat prevention), aggression management, and rockwork requirements. Used as supplementary care guide; no dedicated saulosi page.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pseudotropheus saulosi profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist trade reference. Confirmed sexual dichromatism (yellow females, blue-barred males), dwarf size, Taiwan Reef restriction, mouthbrooding behaviour, beginner-friendliness among mbuna, and hybridisation risk with similar-looking species. Published under old genus Pseudotropheus saulosi.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Chindongo saulosi
additional.notes: Conservation status: Critically Endangered (CR). Threats: extremely restricted range (single reef, Taiwan Reef / Chinyankwazi Island), combined with high ornamental trade collection pressure. Population trend: decreasing. Supports recommendation to source tank-bred specimens.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; spawns readily in a species or mbuna community tank. Female incubates eggs buccally for 21–28 days, releasing 10–25 free-swimming fry. Male coloration emerges at 3–6 months. Recommend isolating brooding females near term to protect fry.
